name: Scandium
name_origin: Named after Scandinavia, from the Latin 'Scandia', because the element was discovered in minerals (euxenite and gadolinite) found in Scandinavian ores.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Scandium is the 50th most abundant element in Earth's crust (~22 ppm) but is widely dispersed and rarely concentrated in ores. The principal source mineral is thortveitite, found mainly in Norway and Madagascar; it is also recovered as a byproduct of uranium and tungsten processing.
states: byproduct of uranium and rare-earth processing

compounds.name: Scandium oxide
compounds.summary: A white solid used as a starting material for other scandium compounds and in high-intensity discharge lamps.

compounds.name: Scandium chloride
compounds.summary: A common scandium salt used as a precursor in catalysis and materials synthesis.

compounds.name: Scandium fluoride
compounds.summary: Notable for its negative thermal expansion and used in optical and ceramic applications.

compounds.name: Scandium nitrate
compounds.summary: Used as a precursor in the production of high-purity Sc2O3 and as a catalyst in some organic reactions.

compounds.name: Scandium iodide
compounds.summary: Added to mercury-vapor lamps to produce a high-efficiency white light closely resembling sunlight.

uses: Aluminum-scandium alloys for aerospace components, sporting goods, and 3D-printed structural parts
Scandium iodide additive in metal-halide lamps for daylight-quality lighting
Stabilizing zirconia in solid oxide fuel cell electrolytes
High-performance bicycle frames, baseball bats, and lacrosse sticks (Al-Sc alloys)
Tracer in oil refining and pipeline studies (radioactive 46Sc)
Research catalysts for organic synthesis (e.g., Sc(OTf)3 Lewis acid)
reactivity_summary: Scandium is a soft, silvery-white transition metal that tarnishes in air to form a yellowish or pinkish oxide coating. It dissolves slowly in most dilute acids but is unreactive with a 1:1 mixture of nitric acid and 48% hydrofluoric acid due to passivation. Scandium reacts with water on heating to release hydrogen.
isotopes_summary: Scandium has only one stable isotope, 45Sc, making it a monoisotopic element. Numerous radioisotopes are known; 46Sc (half-life 83.79 days) is used as a tracer in oil refining and sediment studies.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Scandium
